evidence.description: How does streaming video get to your phone or other streaming device?
evidence.text:
Television & Streaming: How Streaming Works
Special | 6m 6sVideo has Closed Captions
How does streaming video get to your phone or other streaming device?
Many of us watch video on a streaming device more than over-the-air television. How does streaming video work and how is it different from broadcast video?
Problems playing video? | Closed Captioning Feedback
Problems playing video? | Closed Captioning Feedback
Science Trek is a local public television program presented by IdahoPTV
Major Funding by the Laura Moore Cunningham Foundation and the Idaho National Laboratory. Additional Funding by the Friends of Idaho Public Television and the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.
Television & Streaming: How Streaming Works
Special | 6m 6sVideo has Closed Captions
Many of us watch video on a streaming device more than over-the-air television. How does streaming video work and how is it different from broadcast video?
Problems playing video? | Closed Captioning Feedback
How to Watch Science Trek
Science Trek is available to stream on pbs.org and the free PBS App, available on iPhone, Apple TV, Android TV, Android smartphones, Amazon Fire TV, Amazon Fire Tablet, Roku, Samsung Smart TV, and Vizio.
Science Trek
Science Trek is a place where parents, kids, and educators can watch short, educational videos on a variety of science topics. Every Monday Science Trek releases a new video that introduces children to math, science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) career potentials in a fun, informative way.JOAN CARTAN-HANSEN, HOST: While some people watch programs on broadcast television, more watch their phone, tablet or laptops.
Let's learn about streaming.
(Music) GIRL ONE: What are you watching?
GIRL TWO: Some Science Trek.
GIRL ONE: Hey how does the video get to your phone?
GIRL TWO: I'm not sure how it works.
Let's find out.
CARTAN-HANSEN: When you watch broadcast television, your TV is getting a digital signal over the air or through a cable or satellite.
But to get video and sound onto your phone, tablet, laptop, or streaming device on your television, it takes the internet.
And to understand the internet you have to step back in time before computers, before television, before radio.
MAN; Who is that with the deaf boy?
NARRATOR: Him?
Bell's the name.
Professor Alexander Graham Bell.
CARTAN-HANSEN: In 1876, Bell invented the telephone and the first words spoken over the new invention were.
BELL: Mr. Watson, Mr. Watson, come here I want you.
WATSON: Say!
Mr. Bell.
Mr. Bell.
Mr. Bell, I heard every word you said distinctly.
You were perfectly clear.
I recognized your voiced.
You said Mr. Watson, come here I want you.
(laughter) CARTAN-HANSEN: Eventually telephone wires connected the world.
And as computers were invented, they used those telephone lines to send data.
(dial up modem sound) The internet was created in 1969 when scientists from four American universities tied together their computers to share data.
The internet is a worldwide connection of computers all sharing data.
About the same time, wireless networks were invented, so data could be moved through the airwaves.
(Tires Screeching) GIRL 1: Wait, I just want to know how I stream my program to my phone.
CARTAN-HANSEN: Okay, let's ask an engineer.
Dave Starkey is a system performance engineer for Verizon Wireless.
He says it starts with a special app, or program, on your device.
DAVE STARKEY: So basically, when you click the app on your device, the app will then communicate to the server and it'll say, okay, this user wants to watch this movie.
And then the content server and your phone, a data pipe will be connected over the internet, over the network, whether it's a cellular or public internet or WIFI.
And then that movie data stream will start streaming per se, from the server of where the content is being kept to your device.
GIRL 2: A pipe?
CARTAN-HANSEN: Okay, it's not an actual pipe.
It is either a cable, a phone line or, if wireless over a wireless phone connection or WIFI, then its radio waves.
GIRL 1: Okay, then why does the video sometimes stop, and I get that circle thing?
CARTAN-HANSEN: Well, w hen the video stops, there can be a problem..At the provider's server-the data's not getting out or the pipe from the provider is clogged, too much data is being sent, or the pipe to your device isn't big enough to get all the data at once, or your device isn't processing the data right.
STARKEY: Your device is set to where if there's a gap in the clip or just stops, it'll try to buffer, it'll wait.
It's a pause effectively for your device to retrieve the rest of the information that it's waiting for.
So, in this video stream, there's effectively checks as we go along, and if certain checks aren't received, you get that buffering process.
GIRL 2: Is there a difference between streaming and downloading?
STARKEY: Yes.
Downloading is where you would download the data or the content to your phone.
It gets stored on your device and you can watch it there, and then you just delete it when you're done, so you don't fill up your phone.
Streaming.
You have to have an internet connection.
It's a constant back and forth from your device to the server where the content is being held.
It's a constant back and forth.
GIRL 1: What's the difference between television and streaming?
STARKEY: Well, broadcast television, there's a set program schedule.
You know, I know that this show is on at eight in the morning, and if I don't watch it, that show at eight o'clock in the morning, I don't get to see it.
Now with streaming it gives you that on-demand option where I don't have to be tied to my house or I don't have to be tied to a particular schedule.
And that's one of the benefits about streaming.
You can watch things where you want when you want.
Streaming is great that you can pretty much take it with you.
You can watch it when you want, where you want it, but it's also important you put the phone down and enjoy the life that you have.
GIRL ONE: He's right.
GIRL TWO: Okay, basketball or skating?
GIRL ONE: Let's go!
CARTAN-HANSEN: If you want to learn more about television and streaming, check out the Science Trek website.
You'll find it at Science Trek dot org.
(Music) ANNOUNCER: Presentation of Science Trek on Idaho Public Television is made possible through the generous support of the Laura Moore Cunningham Foundation, committed to fulfilling the Moore and Bettis family legacy of building the great state of Idaho, by the Idaho National Laboratory, mentoring talent and finding solutions for energy and security challenges; By the Friends of Idaho Public Television and by the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.
- Science and Nature
Explore scientific discoveries on television's most acclaimed science documentary series.
- Science and Nature
Capturing the splendor of the natural world, from the African plains to the Antarctic ice.

evidence.page_title: Television Broadcast Programs: A Complete Guide
evidence.title: Television Broadcast Programs: A Complete Guide
evidence.description: Television Broadcast Programs: A Complete Guide Alright, guys! Let’s dive deep into the world of television broadcast programs.
evidence.text:
Television Broadcast Programs: A Complete Guide
Alright, guys! Let’s dive deep into the world of television broadcast programs. Ever wondered what makes a TV show tick? Or how your favorite series makes it from the studio to your screen? Well, buckle up because we’re about to break it all down. This comprehensive guide will cover everything from the different types of programs to the nitty-gritty of their production and scheduling. Ready? Let’s get started!
Table of Contents
- Television Broadcast Programs: A Complete Guide
- What are Television Broadcast Programs?
- Types of Television Broadcast Programs
- News Programs
- Entertainment Programs
- Educational Programs
- Documentary Programs
- Reality Shows
- Game Shows
- Sports Broadcasts
- The Production Process of Television Broadcast Programs
- Scheduling of Television Broadcast Programs
- The Impact of Television Broadcast Programs
- The Future of Television Broadcast Programs
What are Television Broadcast Programs?
Television broadcast programs are the heart and soul of the TV industry. These are the shows, series, and specials that are created and distributed for viewing on television. They come in a dazzling array of formats and genres, each designed to cater to different tastes and demographics. Whether it’s the evening news, a gripping drama, a laugh-out-loud comedy, or an educational documentary, each type of program serves a unique purpose. Understanding the breadth of these programs is the first step in appreciating the complex world of television.
Television broadcast programs aim to inform, entertain, educate, or persuade viewers. News programs keep us updated on current events, while entertainment programs like dramas and comedies offer an escape from reality. Educational programs, often found on public broadcasting channels, aim to teach viewers about a particular subject, and documentaries provide in-depth explorations of real-world topics. Then there are reality shows, game shows, sports broadcasts, and a whole host of other formats, each with its own set of conventions and appeals. The diversity of these programs ensures that there’s something for everyone on TV.
Beyond their content, television broadcast programs are also defined by their production values, distribution methods, and scheduling. High-quality production, involving skilled writers, directors, actors, and technical crew, can significantly enhance a program’s appeal. The way a program is distributed—whether through traditional over-the-air broadcasting, cable, satellite, or streaming services—affects its reach and accessibility. Finally, scheduling plays a crucial role in determining a program’s success, as networks strategically place shows in time slots to maximize viewership. In short, television broadcast programs are a complex blend of creativity, technology, and marketing, all working together to capture and hold our attention.
Types of Television Broadcast Programs
Let’s get into the different types of television broadcast programs. Knowing the lay of the land will help you understand the variety available and what makes each one unique. From news to sports, drama to reality, there’s a type for everyone.
News Programs
News programs are all about keeping you informed. These broadcasts cover current events, politics, business, and more. Anchors, reporters, and correspondents work together to deliver up-to-the-minute information, often featuring interviews, on-site reports, and expert analysis. News programs are a staple of television, providing a vital service to the public. Ever wonder how they get that news to you so fast? It’s a combination of skilled journalism and cutting-edge technology, working around the clock to keep you in the loop.
Entertainment Programs
Entertainment programs are designed to, well, entertain! This category includes a wide variety of genres like dramas, comedies, and sitcoms. Dramas tell compelling stories with complex characters and plotlines, often exploring serious themes and conflicts. Comedies and sitcoms, on the other hand, aim to make you laugh, using humor, wit, and relatable situations to lighten your mood. Whether you’re into gripping narratives or lighthearted fun, entertainment programs offer an escape from the everyday.
Educational Programs
Educational programs are all about learning. These broadcasts cover a wide range of subjects, from science and history to art and culture. They often feature experts, demonstrations, and engaging visuals to make learning fun and accessible. Educational programs can be found on public broadcasting channels, as well as on specialized networks dedicated to education. They serve an important role in promoting knowledge and understanding, especially for younger viewers.
Documentary Programs
Documentary programs provide in-depth explorations of real-world topics. These broadcasts delve into subjects like history, science, culture, and social issues, often using interviews, archival footage, and on-site investigations to tell compelling stories. Documentaries can be incredibly informative and thought-provoking, shedding light on important issues and raising awareness. They often take a deep dive into subjects that aren’t typically covered by mainstream media, offering a unique perspective on the world around us.
Reality Shows
Reality shows have become a huge phenomenon in recent years. These broadcasts feature real people in unscripted situations, often competing for a prize or facing challenges. Reality shows can range from talent competitions and dating shows to survival challenges and home improvement programs. The appeal of reality TV lies in its authenticity and the drama that unfolds as real people interact with each other. However, it’s worth noting that many reality shows are heavily edited and produced to create maximum drama and entertainment.
Game Shows
Game shows are all about competition and fun. These broadcasts feature contestants answering questions, solving puzzles, or competing in physical challenges for prizes. Game shows can be incredibly engaging and exciting, offering viewers the chance to play along at home. They often feature charismatic hosts, flashy sets, and dramatic music to heighten the excitement.
Sports Broadcasts
Sports broadcasts bring live sporting events to your TV screen. These broadcasts cover a wide range of sports, from football and basketball to soccer and baseball. Sports broadcasts feature play-by-play commentary, expert analysis, and behind-the-scenes coverage, providing fans with a comprehensive viewing experience. They often include pre-game and post-game shows, as well as highlight reels and interviews with players and coaches.
The Production Process of Television Broadcast Programs
Alright, let’s pull back the curtain and take a look at the production process of television broadcast programs. It’s a complex operation involving a lot of creative minds and technical wizardry.
The production process generally starts with an idea. This could be a pitch for a new TV series, a concept for a documentary, or a plan for a live sports broadcast. Once the idea is approved, the development phase begins. This involves writing scripts, creating storyboards, and planning the overall look and feel of the program. Securing funding is a critical step, as it determines the resources available for production. The scope and budget of the program are carefully considered during this phase.
Once the pre-production phase is complete, the actual filming or recording begins. This involves actors, directors, camera operators, sound technicians, and a whole host of other crew members working together to bring the script to life. For live broadcasts, the production process is even more intense, as everything has to happen in real-time. Technical glitches, unexpected events, and timing issues can all pose challenges. After filming, the post-production phase begins. This involves editing the footage, adding special effects, creating music and sound design, and finalizing the overall look and feel of the program.
Quality control is essential throughout the production process. Every aspect of the program is carefully reviewed to ensure that it meets the required standards. This includes checking for technical errors, ensuring continuity, and verifying that the content is accurate and appropriate. The final step is distribution, which involves getting the program to viewers. This could involve broadcasting it on traditional television channels, streaming it online, or distributing it on DVD or Blu-ray.
Scheduling of Television Broadcast Programs
Let’s talk about the scheduling of television broadcast programs. You might not think about it much, but a lot of thought goes into when and where a show airs. Scheduling is a critical part of television broadcasting, as it directly impacts viewership and revenue.
Networks strategically place shows in time slots to maximize their audience. Prime time, which is typically between 8 PM and 11 PM, is the most valuable time slot, as it attracts the largest number of viewers. Shows that air during prime time often command higher advertising rates and have a greater chance of success. Scheduling decisions are based on a variety of factors, including the target audience, the genre of the program, and the competition from other networks.
Daytime programming, which airs during the morning and afternoon, is typically targeted towards stay-at-home parents and other viewers who are available during those hours. Late-night programming, which airs after 11 PM, often features talk shows, comedies, and other programs aimed at younger audiences. Networks also consider seasonal trends when scheduling programs. For example, they may air holiday-themed shows during the Christmas season or sports events during the summer months.
Promotions and advertising play a key role in driving viewership. Networks use trailers, commercials, and social media to promote their programs and encourage viewers to tune in. They also use cross-promotion, which involves promoting one program during another program, to reach a wider audience. In today’s world, streaming services have changed the scheduling landscape. With on-demand viewing, viewers can watch their favorite shows whenever they want. However, traditional television networks still rely on scheduled programming to attract viewers and generate revenue.
The Impact of Television Broadcast Programs
Okay, let’s explore the impact of television broadcast programs. TV isn’t just something we watch; it shapes our culture, influences our opinions, and reflects our society.
Television broadcast programs have a profound impact on society. They can shape our perceptions of the world, influence our attitudes and beliefs, and even affect our behavior. News programs inform us about current events and help us stay connected to the world around us. Entertainment programs provide us with an escape from reality and offer a shared cultural experience. Educational programs promote learning and understanding, while documentaries shed light on important issues.
Television also plays a role in shaping our cultural identity. It can reinforce stereotypes, promote certain values, and influence our understanding of different cultures and communities. The portrayal of diversity on television is particularly important, as it can help to promote tolerance and understanding. However, it’s also important to be aware of the potential negative impacts of television. Excessive viewing can lead to sedentary behavior, social isolation, and exposure to violence and inappropriate content. Parents and educators need to play a role in helping children and teenagers develop healthy viewing habits and critical thinking skills.
The television industry is constantly evolving, with new technologies and platforms emerging all the time. Streaming services, social media, and interactive television are changing the way we consume television content. As viewers, it’s important to be aware of the impact that television has on our lives and to make informed choices about what we watch. By being critical consumers of television, we can maximize its benefits and minimize its potential harms.
The Future of Television Broadcast Programs
So, what’s next for the future of television broadcast programs? The TV landscape is changing faster than ever, so let’s peek into the crystal ball.
The television industry is undergoing rapid changes. Streaming services, on-demand viewing, and digital technologies are transforming the way we consume television content. Traditional television networks are facing increasing competition from online platforms like Netflix, Amazon Prime Video, and Hulu. To stay relevant, they are adapting to the changing landscape by offering their own streaming services, creating original content for online platforms, and experimenting with new formats and technologies. The rise of streaming services has also led to a shift in viewing habits.
Viewers are increasingly watching television content on their own schedule, rather than tuning in at a specific time to watch a scheduled program. This has led to a decline in traditional television viewership and a rise in binge-watching. Interactive television, which allows viewers to interact with the content they are watching, is also gaining popularity. This could involve voting on reality shows, answering trivia questions, or even controlling the outcome of a story. The future of television broadcast programs is likely to be more personalized, interactive, and on-demand.
Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning are also playing a growing role in the television industry. AI can be used to personalize recommendations, optimize advertising, and even generate content. As technology continues to evolve, the future of television broadcast programs is likely to be shaped by new innovations and trends. The key to success will be adapting to the changing landscape and finding new ways to engage viewers. Whether it’s through traditional television, streaming services, or interactive platforms, the future of television is sure to be exciting and full of possibilities.
There you have it, guys! A complete guide to television broadcast programs. From their types and production to their impact and future, we’ve covered it all. Now you can impress your friends with your TV knowledge!
